DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT FRANCHISE TAX BOARD DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendant Franchise Tax Board's demurrer to the complaint is OVERRULED. Blakemore v. Superior Court (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 36, 53, states: A demurrer to class allegations may be sustained without leave to amend only "where it is clear that there is no reasonable possibility that the plaintiffs could establish a community of interest among the potential class members and that individual issues predominate over common questions of law and fact'Whenever there is a reasonable possibility plaintiffs can plead a prima facie community of interest among class members, the preferred course is to defer decision on the propriety of the class action until an evidentiary hearing has been held on the appropriateness of class litigation." (internal citations and quote marks deleted.)

In this case, it is not clear there is no reasonable possibility that plaintiff or a subset of plaintiffs can establish a community of interest to support class treatment. Defendant's arguments regarding individual issues predominating should be raised when and if plaintiff files a motion for class certification.

Resolution of class issues at the pleading stage is most likely to occur in a mass tort action. (See Tarkington v. California Unemployment Ins. Appeals Bd. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1511.)

Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION TO STAY PROCEEDINGS
Off Calendar May-02-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Defendant Franchise Tax Board's motion to stay proceedings is granted. This case is stayed pending the issuance of the resolution of the appeal in case A140518 currently pending in the First District Court of Appeal. An application for a stay is addressed to the "sound discretion" of the trial court. "In exercising its discretion the court should consider the importance of discouraging multiple litigation designed solely to harass an adverse party, and of avoiding unseemly conflicts with the courts of other jurisdictions. It should also consider whether the rights of the parties can best be determined by the court of the other jurisdiction because of the nature of the subject matter, the availability of witnesses, or the stage to which the proceedings in the other court have already advanced." (Farmland Irr. Co. v. Dopplmaier (1957) 48 Cal.2d 208, 215.) The appeal in A140518 raises the key issue in this case, i.e., whether the class claim administrative scheme of Revenue and Taxation Code 19322 prevents a subsequent civil class action case under CCP 382 involving individual administrative claims. A stay will conserve judicial and party resources and avoid potentially conflicting rulings. Plaintiff fails to highlight any prejudice to it if its action to recover the minimum $800 franchise tax is stayed. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-18-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Amy Silverstein of the law offices of Silverstein & Pomerantz (415.279.4278) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Stewart Pollock of the law offices of Edelson PC (415.212.9300) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Deputy Attorney General, Karen W. Yiu of the California Attorney General's Office (510.879.1245) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Defendant California Franchise Tax Board's Demurrer to Class Action Complaint as to both causes of action in the class action complaint filed by plaintiff The Rasmussen Company, Inc. is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the first cause of action for refund of taxes and overruled as to the second cause of action for declaratory relief. On what appears to be the main issue in dispute between the parties on the first cause of action, the court rules that Rasmussen may seek class relief for a refund without having to allege that each class member's administrative claim was denied by the Board or was deem denied by the class member. Per CCP 382 Rasmussen may seek class relief for a refund on behalf of class members who filed individual administrative claims as long as Rasmussen alleges that each class member exhausted its administrative remedy by making an administrative claim that complied with the requirements of Revenue & Taxation Code 19322 and such claim was either explicitly denied by the Board or more than 180 days elapsed after the claim was made without any action taken by Board. Because Rasmussen's complaint does not explicitly allege that each class member's administrative claim complied with the requirements of section 19322 or limited its class definition to a class comprised solely of taxpayers who made administrative claims that complied with the requirements of section 19322, the Board's demurrer to the first cause of action is sustained and Rasmussen is given leave to cure this deficiency. Rasmussen's second cause of action on its face is not necessarily barred by Article XIII, section 32 of the California Constitution or Revenue & Taxation Code 19381 because one or more of the declarations sought does not inevitably require the State to cease collecting taxes or make a refund of taxes already paid, even if that is the apparent motivation for that claim. Because the need for the second cause of action is unclear in light of the first cause of action, it may become appropriate for the court to address whether that claim should remain for adjudication. (CCP 1061.) However, since the Board has not raised that issue on this demurrer, for the time being it is only flagged for the parties' guidance, and not ruled on. Written order is signed and filed in open court.
